elements.content: This experiment keeps only one reference and two comparison cases: the real circle, the seven-rectangle union, and a seven-rectangle anti-aliased union visualized with shoulder-focused taper bands around the yellow core.

children.content: This sample keeps the prose identical in both sections so the eye can compare only the wrapping lane. The opening lines should step inward gently near the top, hold a broader shoulder through the middle, and then recover the full measure quickly once the obstacle clears.

Because the visible artwork is the same circular disc in both sections, the important question is not whether the image looks round but whether the exclusion lane creates the same reading rhythm. If the line widths and recovery cadence stay close, then a coarse invisible field may already be visually good enough.

This sample keeps the prose identical in both sections so the eye can compare only the wrapping lane. The opening lines should step inward gently near the top, hold a broader shoulder through the middle, and then recover the full measure quickly once the obstacle clears.

Because the visible artwork is the same circular disc in both sections, the important question is not whether the image looks round but whether the exclusion lane creates the same reading rhythm. If the line widths and recovery cadence stay close, then a coarse invisible field may already be visually good enough.

This stress test swaps the circular proof for a rough cat silhouette made entirely from ordinary rectangles. Nothing here is unioned for the wrap lane; the field is published as a literal non-union assembly so we can see how the same substrate behaves once the authored shape becomes more irregular.

If the wrap still feels stable, then the implication is strong: rectangles can serve as a practical authoring surface even for more expressive silhouettes, with refinement happening later only where it helps. The page does not need to know that the cat began as boxes. It only needs a believable exclusion field.
